title: Oklahoma Baptists Pass Resolution Demanding an Immediate "End" to Abortion
author: James Silberman
orientation: At the 2019 Baptist General Convention of Oklahoma, messengers passed an amended Resolution 7 calling on the legislature to enact legislation for the immediate end of abortion without exception or compromise. Free the States celebrates the grassroots Southern Baptist push while warning that the word 'end' leaves wiggle room and that BGCO leaders may still try to substitute heartbeat or Alabama-style bills for true abolition.
audiences: Southern Baptist pastor weighing whether to push for abolition in his denomination
Pro-life activist trying to understand the abolitionist critique of incremental bills
Oklahoma legislator deciding whether to support SB13
Denominational leader (BGCO executive) facing pressure from the grassroots
Lay churchgoer trying to discern between 'pro-life' and 'abolitionist' language

questions.question: What concrete action did the messengers at the 2019 Baptist General Convention of Oklahoma take regarding abortion?
questions.audience: Southern Baptist pastor weighing whether to push for abolition in his denomination
questions.answer: Messengers voted overwhelmingly to amend Resolution 7 with explicit language directing the state legislature to end abortion immediately, transforming a typical proclamation into a directive to lawmakers.
questions.quote: Representatives of the assembled Southern Baptist churches voted overwhelmingly in favor of a resolution calling for an immediate end of abortion in Oklahoma.

questions.question: What language did Pastor Bill Ascol's amendment add to Resolution 7?
questions.audience: Southern Baptist pastor weighing whether to push for abolition in his denomination
questions.answer: Ascol's amendment added a final sentence directing the Oklahoma State Legislature to pass legislation ending abortion immediately, with no exceptions and no compromise.
questions.quote: Finally, we call upon the Oklahoma State Legislature to enact legislation that calls for the immediate end of abortion without exception or compromise.

questions.question: Why did Brett Baggett and other pastors feel the original Resolution 7 was insufficient?
questions.audience: Lay churchgoer trying to discern between 'pro-life' and 'abolitionist' language
questions.answer: Baggett described the original as a typical pro-life statement with no specific directive, more declaration than action item, and the pastors wanted a resolution that explicitly told legislators to pass a bill ending abortion immediately.
questions.quote: Resolution 7 was a typical pro-life resolution. It read more like a proclamation than a resolution. So a few pastors and myself who are from Oklahoma and are part of the Southern Baptist Convention – we came together to propose an amendment to try to make it more explicitly say something like calling the legislators in Oklahoma to pass legislation that would immediately end abortion.

questions.question: What sparked grassroots Southern Baptist opposition to BGCO leadership in the first place?
questions.audience: Oklahoma legislator deciding whether to support SB13
questions.answer: In February 2019, BGCO leaders publicly opposed SB13, the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act, which prompted pastors across the state to organize, hold a roundtable, and rally at the capitol declaring that the BGCO did not speak for them.
questions.quote: The inspiration for the resolution was instigated in February by BGCO leaders when they announced their opposition to SB13, the Abolition of Abortion in Oklahoma Act. Southern Baptist pastors around the state were stunned and responded by proclaiming that BGCO leadership did not speak for them.

questions.question: Why does Free the States consider 'end' a weaker word choice than 'abolish' in the resolution?
questions.audience: Pro-life activist trying to understand the abolitionist critique of incremental bills
questions.answer: 'End' does not specify a mechanism, allowing pro-life people who only want abortion to become 'unthinkable' (rather than illegal) to sign on, whereas 'abolish,' 'outlaw,' or 'criminalize' name the legal mechanism and tie the resolution to actual abolition legislation like SB13.
questions.quote: The word "end" lacks specificity regarding the mechanism by which abortion is to end, which in a formal document establishing a policy position, is important. There are a lot of pro-life people who say things like, "I don't want abortion to illegal, I want it to be unthinkable." Such people could agree that abortion should end even though they don't agree that abortion should be abolished.

questions.question: What is wrong, in the article's view, with the Alabama-style bill that BGCO leaders may try to substitute for SB13?
questions.audience: Oklahoma legislator deciding whether to support SB13
questions.answer: The Alabama bill purports to end abortion from conception but does not criminalize abortion as murder, gives immunity to mothers who have their child killed, and only takes effect pending Supreme Court approval, so it remains unenforced while Planned Parenthood expands.
questions.quote: Bills like this one which give immunity to the parents who have their child murdered (thus depriving preborn human beings of equal protection and legalizing self-induced abortion) and/or submit to the Supreme Court's evil and unconstitutional opinion on abortion are anathema to abolitionism and to God's word upon which abolitionism is based.

questions.question: What two features does Free the States identify as the distinguishing test of a true abolition bill versus a pro-life bill?
questions.audience: Pro-life activist trying to understand the abolitionist critique of incremental bills
questions.answer: A true abolition bill must (1) fully criminalize abortion as homicide for everyone involved and (2) defy the Supreme Court rather than submitting to its rulings; bills lacking either feature are designed to deceive sincere supporters of abolition.
questions.quote: Full criminalization of abortion as homicide for all involved in the murder and defiance of the Supreme Court are the two primary hangups for people regarding the abolition of abortion, so this new bill would likely lack one or both of these aspects.

questions.question: Which BGCO leaders are named as wielding the institutional power that could either honor or sidestep the resolution?
questions.audience: Denominational leader (BGCO executive) facing pressure from the grassroots
questions.answer: Hance Dilbeck (Executive Director), Blake Gideon (President), and Brian Hobbs (Editor of The Baptist Messenger) are identified as the three leaders whose response will determine whether the convention's stated position is institutionally upheld.
questions.quote: Hopefully, Oklahoma Baptist leaders Hance Dilbeck (Executive Director), Blake Gideon (President), and Brian Hobbs (Editor of *The Baptist Messenger*), who wield immense political power, adhere to their convention's agreed upon principles.

questions.question: How does the article characterize the difference between denominational leaders and the grassroots on abolition?
questions.audience: Denominational leader (BGCO executive) facing pressure from the grassroots
questions.answer: Like many institutions, the leaders are politically cautious and have submitted to worldly wisdom and the Supreme Court, while the rank-and-file pastors and members are calling them back to a righteous standard.
questions.quote: As is the case with many institutions, the leaders of Oklahoma Baptists are less willing to stand up for what's right when it's politically risky than the grassroots. Whereas the convention's leaders have operated thus far on worldly wisdom and have submitted to the Supreme Court rather than God, many Southern Baptist pastors and churches are calling the leaders of the Oklahoma Baptists back to a righteous standard.

questions.question: What other Oklahoma denomination had already passed an abolitionist resolution earlier in 2019?
questions.audience: Lay churchgoer trying to discern between 'pro-life' and 'abolitionist' language
questions.answer: The Oklahoma Free Will Baptists, in May 2019, passed a resolution demanding the total and immediate abolition of abortion, introduced by Pastor Tim Gillespie of Stroud Free Will Baptist Church.
questions.quote: In passing this resolution, Southern Baptists join Oklahoma Free Will Baptists who, in May, passed a resolution demanding the total and immediate abolition of abortion. It was introduced by pastor Tim Gillespie of Stroud Free Will Baptist Church.

questions.question: Does Free the States believe abolition depends on getting BGCO leadership on board?
questions.audience: Denominational leader (BGCO executive) facing pressure from the grassroots
questions.answer: No. The author welcomes their cooperation but says the movement will succeed regardless because Christians, pastors, and entire congregations across denominations are mobilizing for immediate abolition.
questions.quote: But if they do not change course and remain anti-abolitionist, they will not ultimately be needed to abolish abortion. Christians, pastors, and entire congregations of various Christian denominations are getting off the sidelines and making their call for the immediate abolition of abortion as murder heard. Abortion is going to be abolished in Oklahoma with or without Dilbeck, Gideon, and Hobbs.

related.title: The Three Pro-Life Men Keeping Abortion Legal in Oklahoma
related.reason: Profiles the BGCO and pro-life establishment figures whose February 2019 opposition to SB13 set the stage for this resolution fight.

related.title: Pastor and Gubernatorial Candidate Dan Fisher Responds to Oklahoma Baptist Leaders Opposing SB13
related.reason: Direct rebuttal to the BGCO leadership's anti-SB13 letter that the resolution was designed to repudiate.

related.title: Uncovered Letter Exposes Plan to Keep Abortion Legal in Oklahoma
related.reason: Documents the broader institutional strategy by pro-life leaders to keep abortion legal that this resolution pushes back against.

related.title: Prominent Southern Baptist Voice Calls For Total Abolition
related.reason: Follow-up coverage of Bill Ascol, the pastor who introduced the amendment to Resolution 7, continuing his abolitionist witness inside the SBC.

related.title: Todd Fisher Elected President of OK Baptists. A Step Toward or Away From Abolition?
related.reason: Updates the BGCO leadership story one year later, evaluating whether the new president would honor the 2019 resolution.

related.title: Southern Baptist Convention Moves One Step Closer to Abolitionism (And Answering Objections)
related.reason: Tracks how the grassroots Southern Baptist push for abolition documented here scaled up to the national SBC level in 2021.

related.title: Tennessee Fetal Heartbeat Bill—A Step in the Wrong Direction
related.reason: Develops the article's warning about heartbeat and Alabama-style bills being deceptive substitutes for true abolition legislation.
